statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
Roots change, but the tree stands strong — so should your logic.

Behruzbek received a tree $ ^{\text{∗}} $ with $ n $ nodes. For a chosen root $ ^{\text{†}} $ $ r $ , Behruzbek wants to find cuteness of the tree.

Consider every set of $ k $ distinct nodes of the tree. For each such set, compute its lowest common ancestor ([LCA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lowest_common_ancestor)) in the tree when it is rooted at $ r $ . Let $ S_r $ be the set of all distinct nodes obtained this way; then cuteness of the tree is $ |S_r| $ , where $ |S| $ means the number of distinct elements.

After discovering the cuteness of trees, Behruzbek became interested in finding the kawaiiness of the tree! Kawaiiness is defined as:

$$$
\sum_{r = 1}^{n} |S_r| = |S_1| + |S_2| + \dots + |S_n|
$$$

Unfortunately, Behruzbek is feeling sleepy now. Please help Behruzbek by finding the kawaiiness of the tree!

$ ^{\text{∗}} $ A tree is a connected graph without cycles.

$ ^{\text{†}} $ A rooted tree is a tree where one vertex is special and called the root.

Input Format
The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \leq t \leq 10^{4} $ ).

The first line of each test case contains two integers $ n $ and $ k $ ( $ 2 \leq k \leq n \leq 2\cdot 10^{5} $ ) — the number of vertices in the tree and the number of distinct integers to be chosen.

The following $ n-1 $ lines of each test case describe the tree. Each of the lines contains two integers $ u $ and $ v $ ( $ 1 \leq u,v \leq n $ , $ u \ne v $ ) that indicate an edge between vertex $ u $ and $ v $ . It is guaranteed that these edges form a tree.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n $ over all test cases does not exceed $ 2\cdot 10^{5} $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output one integer — the value of $ \sum\limits_{r=1}^n |S_r| $ .

Examples
Sample 1
Input
4
2 2
1 2
5 3
1 2
1 3
1 4
1 5
6 3
1 2
1 3
2 4
2 5
3 6
10 5
5 6
4 9
3 9
2 6
2 8
8 9
6 10
1 6
4 7
Output
2
9
17
35

Note
Let $ f(i) = |S_i| $

For the third example:

- Root is $ 1 $ , only $ 1 $ and $ 2 $ nodes can be obtained. For example, we can choose: $ LCA(4, 5, 6) = 1 $ and $ LCA(2, 4, 5) = 2 $ . As a result, $ f(1) = 2 $ .
- Root is $ 2 $ , only $ 1 $ and $ 2 $ nodes can be obtained. For example, we can choose: $ LCA(1, 3, 6) = 1 $ and $ LCA(1, 4, 5) = 2 $ . As a result, $ f(2) = 2 $ .
- Root is $ 3 $ , $ f(3) = 3 $ . For example, node $ 3 $ can be obtained by choosing: $ LCA(2, 4, 6) = 3 $ .
- Root is $ 4 $ , $ f(4) = 3 $ . For example, node $ 2 $ can be obtained by choosing: $ LCA(1, 3, 5) = 2 $ .
- Root is $ 5 $ , $ f(5) = 3 $ . For example, node $ 2 $ can be obtained by choosing: $ LCA(3, 4, 6) = 2 $ .
- Root is $ 6 $ , $ f(6) = 4 $ . For example, node $ 3 $ can be obtained by choosing: $ LCA(3, 4, 5) = 2 $ .

Overall, $ 2 + 2 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 4 = 17 $ .

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content:
We don't need to know
lowest common ancestor (LCA)
to solve this problem. Think of subtree sizes to replace
LCA
.

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.content: Let $$$sz_{i} = $$$ subtree size of $$$i$$$ when root is $$$r$$$. In order for $$$i$$$ to be able to be in the $$$S_{r}$$$: $$$sz_{i} \geq k$$$ must hold.

hints.title: Hint 3
hints.content: Notice how instead of calculating the answer for each root, we can calculate contribution of $$$i$$$ when root is $$$1 \ldots n$$$.

hints.title: Hint for bonus 1
hints.content: Let us have the answer for $$$dp_{v} = $$$ answer when root is $$$v$$$, let $$$u$$$ be the directly connected node to $$$v$$$. How $$$dp_{v}$$$ changes when we make root $$$u$$$ ($$$dp_{u}$$$)?

solutions.title: Solution
solutions.content:
2167F - Tree, TREE!!!
Let us define $$$sz_{i}$$$ = subtree size of $$$i$$$ and $$$f(i) = |S_i|$$$.
Let's first proof why
LCA
is connected to the $$$sz_{i}$$$. If $$$sz_{i} \geq k$$$, it implies that we can choose $$$k - 1$$$ nodes in the subtree of $$$i$$$ other than $$$i$$$ and $$$i$$$ itself, it gives $$$i$$$ as a result.
Now that we know the condition, our task is now just to derive the formula to calculate. Instead of calculating $$$f(i)$$$ independently, we will focus on the contribution of node $$$i$$$ in the sum.
To calculate the contribution of $$$i$$$ in the sum, it is equal to checking, in how many roots($$$1 \ldots n$$$), $$$sz_{i} \geq k$$$ holds. We will divide it into two parts: subtree of $$$i$$$ and outside of subtree of $$$i$$$. If $$$sz_{i} \geq k$$$, then we can choose any node outside of subtree of $$$i$$$ as a root, and still $$$sz_{i} \geq k$$$ holds, because we are not changing subtree itself. Now let's consider outside of it, if $$$n - sz_{i} \geq k$$$ holds, then we can choose any node in the subtree of $$$i$$$ as a root, and still $$$sz_{i} \geq k$$$ holds.

solutions.title: Solution for bonus
solutions.content:
First, we calculate the answer for each $$$i$$$ (the subtree of $$$i$$$) and subtree sizes, where the starting point is arbitrary.
Let's go back to our hint. Suppose we already have the answer for $$$dp_{v}$$$.
To get the answer for $$$dp_{u}$$$, we need to understand how the answer changes based on the subtree of $$$u$$$ and the part outside it.
This technique is known as
Rerooting
. When we transition from $$$v$$$ to $$$u$$$, we divide the total contribution into two parts:
1. inside the subtree of $$$u$$$, and
2. outside the subtree of $$$u$$$.
If $$$n - sz_{u} \lt k$$$, then we should subtract that contribution from $$$dp_{v}$$$ — this corresponds to the part outside the subtree of $$$u$$$, because when $$$u$$$ becomes root, $$$v$$$ have no longer subtree of $$$u$$$ as a subtree, which implies that we need to remove contribution of it. As for the subtree of $$$u$$$: if $$$sz_{u} \lt k$$$, then the answer increases by one because $$$n \ge k$$$.
Finally, the transition becomes:
$$$ dp_{u} = dp_{v} - (n - sz_{u}  \lt  k) + (sz_{u}  \lt  k) $$$
